title: Sustainability Reporting in Europe: From Compliance to Strategy
gist: The move to ESRS Set 2 (FY2026), which cuts required sustainability data points by 60-70%, gives European companies a one-time reset to shift from costly, overreporting compliance toward strategy-led 'smart compliance' — while AI in reporting remains stuck at experimentation rather than transformation.

insights.claim: ESRS Set 2, applicable from FY2026 onward, reduces the number of required sustainability data points by approximately 60% to 70% and eliminates certain voluntary disclosures, shifting the emphasis from completeness to decision usefulness.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: A 60-70% data-point cut materially lowers compliance cost and reshapes what reporting-software and assurance vendors can sell into FY2026 cycles.
insights.quote: The revised standards reduce the number of mandatory data points by approximately 60% to 70% and eliminate certain voluntary disclosures.
insights.locator: The subsequent development of ESRS Set 2

insights.claim: FY2026 will be the first reporting cycle that fully enables a reimagined, strategy-led sustainability reporting approach, making it a one-time inflection point since the introduction of CSRD.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Watch FY2026 disclosures as the test of whether companies actually restructure reporting or just trim it at the margins.
insights.quote: For the first time since the introduction of CSRD, organizations are in a position to fundamentally reimagine their sustainability reporting.
insights.locator: This change presents companies with a unique inflection point

insights.claim: The EU Omnibus Package, introduced in early 2025, extended CSRD implementation timelines and reduced the scope of the regulation, preceding the more substantive ESRS Set 2 adjustment.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Signals a clear EU policy direction of regulatory simplification/rollback on ESG disclosure that affects compliance-vendor demand and corporate reporting burden.
insights.quote: The initial EU Omnibus Package, introduced in early 2025, extended implementation timelines and reduced the scope of the regulation.
insights.locator: Recognizing these challenges

insights.claim: ESRS Set 2 places stronger emphasis on an explicit top-down application of double materiality, letting companies start from their strategic understanding of the business rather than exhaustively cataloging potential sustainability topics.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Top-down materiality should shrink the topic list and reporting burden, favoring firms that can articulate a coherent sustainability strategy.
insights.quote: ESRS Set 2 places stronger emphasis on a more explicit top-down perspective, allowing companies to use their strategic understanding of the business as a starting point to identify material topics.
insights.locator: Sustainability Strategy: Reestablishing the Linkage

insights.claim: Recurring annual sustainability reporting costs for large organizations exceed €1 million and can be significantly higher once the full scope of internal and external effort is counted.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Quantifies the addressable spend that simplification and AI enablement could compress — a headwind for compliance-service revenue, tailwind for efficiency tooling.
insights.quote: Recurring annual costs for large organizations exceed €1 million and can be significantly higher when taking into account the full scope of internal and external endeavors.
insights.locator: As a result, sustainability reporting has evolved

insights.claim: The prescriptive structure of ESRS Set 1 drove companies to adopt broad interpretations of materiality, producing overreporting that severed the intended link between disclosure and strategic priorities.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: The regulation's design produced perverse behavior — evidence that mandatory disclosure regimes can reduce, not increase, decision usefulness.
insights.quote: Faced with a highly prescriptive framework, many companies opt for a broad interpretation of materiality, resulting in disclosures that cover a multitude of topics.
insights.locator: More importantly, the structure of the current standards

insights.claim: Most companies still cover a broad range of material topics that are only loosely connected to their business model, and many material topics are only partially backed by concrete targets or incentive schemes.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Disconnect between reported topics and actual targets is a red flag for investors reading current sustainability reports as strategy signals.
insights.quote: This is particularly apparent from the number of material sustainability topics that companies cover in their reporting that are only partially covered by concrete targets or incentive schemes.
insights.locator: A Compliance-Driven Equilibrium

insights.claim: Sustainability reporting under ESRS has become a cross-functional process requiring input from sustainability, finance, risk, IT, and legal teams plus external advisors and assurance providers, spanning complex value chains.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: The breadth of internal stakeholders explains why costs are high and why organizational redesign, not just software, is the bottleneck.
insights.locator: As a result, sustainability reporting has evolved into a cross-functional process

insights.claim: The dominant market equilibrium across industries treats sustainability reporting primarily as a compliance exercise rather than a strategic tool, a pattern BCG classifies via distinct archetypes.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: If reporting is compliance theater industry-wide, the disclosures carry limited alpha for stock-picking on sustainability performance.
insights.quote: Most companies approach sustainability reporting primarily as a compliance exercise.
insights.locator: A Compliance-Driven Equilibrium

insights.claim: EFRAG's State of Play study, published in early July 2026, analyzed more than 900 FY2025 reports against a structured framework of 18 key questions covering materiality assessment, target setting, and report structure.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: A 900+ report dataset is the current empirical baseline for how European firms disclose — useful reference for benchmarking reporting quality.
insights.quote: The latest State of Play study from the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), published in early July 2026 and more than 900 reports for FY2025
insights.locator: Challenge: Sustainability Reporting Between Market Practice and Regulatory Intent

insights.claim: Because the >900 FY2025 reports were prepared under ESRS Set 1, they reflect current market practice rather than the intended future state under ESRS Set 2, meaning today's disclosure quality understates the post-reform trajectory.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Don't extrapolate current overreporting forward — the FY2026 regime is designed to look materially different.
insights.locator: Because these reports were prepared under the existing ESRS Set 1

insights.claim: Despite vendor and advisor narratives positioning AI as a game changer for sustainability reporting, actual deployment remains limited in scope and impact, contributing more to experimentation at the margins than transformation at scale.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: A sober counter to AI-in-ESG-tooling hype — near-term revenue from these products likely lags the marketing.
insights.quote: AI in sustainability reporting today contributes less to transformation at scale and more to experimentation at the margins.
insights.locator: AI as a Promised Step Change, but One That Has Yet to Materialize

insights.claim: The AI adoption gap in sustainability reporting is driven by four factors: use cases misaligned with actual pain points, fragmented data and weak data governance, poor integration into reporting workflows leaving isolated pilots, and lagging organizational readiness.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Winners in reporting-AI will be those solving data governance and workflow integration, not those shipping standalone models.
insights.locator: Several underlying factors help explain this gap

insights.claim: Emerging agentic AI solutions aim to orchestrate end-to-end reporting workflows with limited human intervention, but current applications frequently fail to deliver anticipated efficiency gains or quality improvements.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Agentic reporting is still forward-looking; treat vendor claims of autonomous reporting as unproven for now.
insights.quote: Many current applications fail to achieve anticipated efficiency gains or quality improvements.
insights.locator: AI as a Promised Step Change

insights.claim: Realizing AI's value in sustainability reporting requires embedding it into the end-to-end process on high-impact use cases rather than running isolated pilots, plus targeted capability building for sustainability, finance, and data teams.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Process integration and skills, not model sophistication, are the gating factors for AI ROI in this domain.
insights.locator: Enablers: Embedding AI and Strengthening Governance

insights.claim: BCG proposes a 'smart compliance' three-layer model — sustainability strategy, an end-to-end reporting process, and enablers (AI, governance, organization) — to shift firms from compliance-driven to strategy-led reporting without reducing rigor.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: The framework is the consulting product BCG is selling around the FY2026 reset; a template for what advisory demand will pitch.
insights.quote: Smart compliance does not entail less rigor. Rather, it reflects a more focused and structured approach that prioritizes material, decision-useful disclosures while minimizing unnecessary complexity and effort.
insights.locator: Proposed Solution: A Strategy-Led Sustainability Reporting Model

insights.claim: Smart compliance structures sustainability reporting into seven interconnected steps: scope and governance, materiality scoping, disclosure/KPI design, data collection, calculation and roll-up, narrative/review/assurance, and publication with continuous improvement.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: A concrete operating blueprint firms can benchmark their reporting maturity against.
insights.locator: A smart compliance approach tackles these challenges

insights.claim: The proposal calls for making sustainability numbers 'finance-grade' through defined calculation methodologies, systematic reconciliations, restatement mechanisms, and version control to ensure auditability.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Rising assurance expectations mean sustainability data is converging toward financial-reporting controls — relevant for audit and assurance providers.
insights.locator: Calculation, Roll-up, and Control

insights.claim: Many organizations still run fragmented reporting setups with siloed responsibilities, multiple handovers, and heavy manual intervention, which raise cost and introduce inconsistencies that weaken controls and complicate assurance.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: The operational mess is the value pool — automation and process redesign target these handovers directly.
insights.locator: Building on a clear strategic foundation

insights.claim: Effective sustainability reporting models combine centralized coordination (a dedicated reporting function or steering body) with distributed ownership of data and content across business units, and governance must evolve to oversee AI model use and output validation.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Organizational design, including AI oversight, is becoming part of audit-readiness — a governance gap for firms scaling reporting.
insights.locator: Effective models typically combine centralized coordination

insights.claim: Companies that fail to use the FY2026 reset risk becoming locked into inefficient processes with continuing cost pressure, missing the chance to turn sustainability reporting into a source of competitive advantage.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: The reform creates a divergence between reporting leaders and laggards worth tracking as an operational-efficiency signal.
insights.quote: Inaction, on the other hand, could carry the risk of further entrenching inefficient processes.
insights.locator: Executive Summary

insights.claim: A robust double materiality assessment should combine top-down strategic judgment with structured bottom-up validation, incorporating civil society and NGO perspectives as a counterbalance to internal strategy that may be incomplete.
insights.speaker: Lorenzo Fantini
insights.so_what: Guards against top-down materiality merely rationalizing the status quo — a quality marker for credible disclosures.
insights.quote: external stakeholder perspectives—including those from civil society and NGOs—offer a critical counterbalance.
insights.locator: At the same time, any top-down perspective
